title: Part 3 – Resident of another country
description: Use this part if you were a resident of a country other than Australia or Timor-Leste for tax purposes during 2016–17.

content_blocks.text: You will need details of your JPDA income from your PAYG payment summary – foreign employment for 2016–17, or a letter from your employer.

Your net income earned in the JPDA is taxed in Australia. You can claim a tax offset of 90% of Australian tax payable on that income. Your payer should have withheld 10% of the applicable Australian tax on your JPDA income.
Show all your Australian income (including all JPDA income) and deductions as instructed by myTax. Use worksheet 4 or worksheet 5 to work out your other non-refundable tax offset.
Use worksheet 4 if the only Australian income you had was JPDA income; otherwise, use worksheet 5 . If you are using worksheet 5 , example 3 will assist you.
Worksheet 4: JPDA tax offset for foreign residents whose only Australian income is JPDA income

rows: Your taxable income as shown in myTax
Calculate your tax using the rates and calculators

content_blocks.text: The amount at row d is your JPDA tax offset. Enter this amount at 'Other non-refundable tax offset' in Foreign employment income (on a payment summary) in myTax.

Gavin, a chef, was a resident of Malaysia for the whole year. His Australian assessable income was $80,000, of which $70,000 was JPDA income. He paid $100 for work-related expenses related to earning his JPDA income but had no other allowable deductions. He had no other amount to show at Other non-refundable tax offsets. Gavin uses worksheet 5 below to work out his JPDA tax offset.

rows: Your taxable income as shown in myTax
Calculate your tax using the rates and calculators .
Divide row b by row a (round to 3 decimal places).
Net JPDA income (after any allowable deductions relating to JPDA income)
Multiply row c by row d .

content_blocks.text: Gavin’s JPDA tax offset is $20,445. He enters this amount at 'Other non-refundable tax offset' in Foreign employment income (on a payment summary) in myTax.
Worksheet 5: JPDA tax offset for foreign residents who have JPDA income and other Australian income

rows: Your taxable income as shown in myTax
Calculate your tax using the rates and calculators .
Divide row b by row a (round to 3 decimal places).
Net JPDA income (after any allowable deductions relating to JPDA income)
Multiply row c by row d .

content_blocks.text: The amount at row g is your JPDA offset. Enter this amount at 'Other non-refundable tax offset' in Foreign employment income (on a payment summary) in myTax.

content_text:
What you need
You will need details of your JPDA income from your PAYG payment summary – foreign employment for 2016–17, or a letter from your employer.
## You need to know
Your net income earned in the JPDA is taxed in Australia. You can claim a tax offset of 90% of Australian tax payable on that income. Your payer should have withheld 10% of the applicable Australian tax on your JPDA income.
## What you need to do
Show all your Australian income (including all JPDA income) and deductions as instructed by myTax. Use worksheet 4 or worksheet 5 to work out your other non-refundable tax offset.
Use worksheet 4 if the only Australian income you had was JPDA income; otherwise, use worksheet 5 . If you are using worksheet 5 , example 3 will assist you.
### Worksheet 4: JPDA tax offset for foreign residents whose only Australian income is JPDA income
Row | Calculation elements | Amount
a | Your taxable income as shown in myTax | $
b | Calculate your tax using the rates and calculators | $
c | Multiply row b by 90 | $
d | Divide row c by 100 | $
The amount at row d is your JPDA tax offset. Enter this amount at 'Other non-refundable tax offset' in Foreign employment income (on a payment summary) in myTax.
Example 3
Gavin, a chef, was a resident of Malaysia for the whole year. His Australian assessable income was $80,000, of which $70,000 was JPDA income. He paid $100 for work-related expenses related to earning his JPDA income but had no other allowable deductions. He had no other amount to show at Other non-refundable tax offsets. Gavin uses worksheet 5 below to work out his JPDA tax offset.
Row | Calculation elements | Amount
a | Your taxable income as shown in myTax | $79,900
b | Calculate your tax using the rates and calculators . | $25,967.50
c | Divide row b by row a (round to 3 decimal places). | 0.325
d | Net JPDA income (after any allowable deductions relating to JPDA income) | $69,900
e | Multiply row c by row d . | $22,717.50
f | Multiply row e by 90. | $2,044,575.00
g | Divide row f by 100. | $20,445.75
Gavin’s JPDA tax offset is $20,445. He enters this amount at 'Other non-refundable tax offset' in Foreign employment income (on a payment summary) in myTax.
### Worksheet 5: JPDA tax offset for foreign residents who have JPDA income and other Australian income
Row | Calculation elements | Amount
a | Your taxable income as shown in myTax | $
b | Calculate your tax using the rates and calculators . | $
c | Divide row b by row a (round to 3 decimal places). | 
d | Net JPDA income (after any allowable deductions relating to JPDA income) | $
e | Multiply row c by row d . | $
f | Multiply row e by 90. | $
g | Divide row f by 100. | $
The amount at row g is your JPDA offset. Enter this amount at 'Other non-refundable tax offset' in Foreign employment income (on a payment summary) in myTax.
